### 1D Features

The primary sequence of human ORAI1 (301 aa) exhibits the characteristic architecture of a store-operated calcium channel pore-forming subunit: four transmembrane helices that assemble to form the central ion-conducting pore of the CRAC channel [@prakriya2006; @hou2012]. Among these segments, TM1 forms the channel pore and contains the highly conserved Glu106 residue, plays a key role in the strong Ca²⁺ selectivity of the channel @prakriya2006. 

Furthermore, extracellular loops provide key regulatory sites: the first loop (TM1-TM2) attracts Ca²⁺, while the third contains the glycosylation site N223, modulating lectin interactions @Humer2022. Cysteine residues (C126, C143, C195) mediate redox regulation, where H₂O₂ exerts inhibitory effects [@soboloff2012; @uniprot_orai1_2024]. 

#### **Transmembrane topology (DeepTMHMM)**

In order to predict the transmembrane topology of ORAI1, the [DeepTMHMM server](https://dtu.biolib.com/DeepTMHMM) was used, as is a well-known deep-learning-based tool designed specifically to identify transmembrane helices with high accuracy from the aminoacid sequence. 

The analysis classified ORAI1 as a transmembrane protein containing four transmembrane helices, consistent with the topology described for CRAC channel subunits. Specifically, the helices were located at residues 92-105, 120-140, 175-196 and 234-255. 
In adittion, both the N-terminal (residues 92-105) and the C-terminal (residues 256-301) regions, and the loop connecting TM2-TM3, are located in the cytoplasmic side, whereas the loops that link TM1-TM2 and TM3-TM4 are predicted to be located on the extracellular side.
This topology matches with the known organization of ORAI1 in the membrane and is related with its function as the pore of the CRAC channel [@prakriya2006; @hou2012].

The high posterior probabilities observed for all transmembrane segments (@fig-DeepTMHMM-ORAI1) indicate a good prediction. This means that these regions are likely to be predicted with higher confidence than the loops regions.

![**DeepTMHMM Most Likely Topology and Posterior probabilities for ORAI1**](pics/ORAI1/ORAI1_DeepTMHMM.png){#fig-DeepTMHMM-ORAI1}

#### **Secondary structure prediction (PSIPRED)**

Secondary structure prediction of the ORAI1 protein was performed using [PSIPRED](https://bioinf.cs.ucl.ac.uk/psipred/) in order to confirm that the previously predicted transmembrane segments correspond to α-helical structures, while the remaining regions are mainly predicted to adopt coil conformations.

The analysis, represented in @fig-PSIPRED-ORAI1, shows that the N-terminal region mainly adopts coil structures, suggesting a cytoplasmic segment. The central part contains four well-defined α-helices that match the transmembrane regions predicted by DeepTMHMM, separated by short coil loops. The C-terminal region is also mostly predicted as coil, supporting its role as a flexible cytoplasmic domain.

![**ORAI1 secondary structure prediction by PSIPRED**](pics/ORAI1/ORAI1_PSIPRED.png){#fig-PSIPRED-ORAI1}

#### **Intrinsic disorder prediction (IUPred3)**

To predict the propensity of each amino acid to be located in intrinsically disordered regions, [IUPred3](https://iupred3.elte.hu/) was used. The results are shown in @fig-IUPred3-ORAI1.

![**Orai1 intrinsic disorder prediction by IUPred3**](pics/ORAI1/ORAI1_IUPred3.png){#fig-IUPred3-ORAI1}

The N-terminal region is highly flexible, the central region alternates structured transmembrane domains with flexible loops, and the C-terminal region shows moderate flexibility, consistent with its regulatory role.

#### **Solvent accessibility (NetSurfP)**

Additional structural features were analysed using [NetSurfP](https://services.healthtech.dtu.dk/services/NetSurfP-3.0/) to evaluate solvent accessibility along the ORAI1 sequence (@fig-NetSurfP-ORAI1). 

![**Orai1 solvent accesibility prediction by NetSurfP**](/pics/ORAI1/ORAI1_NetSurfP.png){#fig-NetSurfP-ORAI1}

The N-terminal region is highly solvent-exposed, indicating a flexible cytoplasmic segment. In contrast, the central transmembrane regions are buried and show low accessibility values, with short exposed loops connecting them. These regions also display very high α-helix probabilities, consistent with their transmembrane nature. Finally, the C-terminal region shows higher accessibility and lower helix propensity, supporting its role as a flexible cytoplasmic regulatory domain.

#### **Combined 1D analysis**

In conclusion, the combined 1D analysis reveals that ORAI1 contains four transmembrane α-helices supported by topology, secondary structure, and accessibility predictions, forming a structured membrane-embedded core. In contrast, the N-terminal and C-terminal regions show higher disorder and solvent exposure, indicating flexible cytoplasmic domains. Overall, this organization is consistent with the typical architecture of ORAI calcium channels.
